planting.note: Sow in warm soil; tolerates heat and some drought well.

directions: Sow directly about an inch deep once soil has warmed, thinning to a few inches apart. This early, heat-tolerant bean produces slightly curved, stringless pods in a concentrated flush. Begin picking when pods are pencil-thick and snap crisply.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when soil reaches at least 60 degrees.

directions: Push seeds an inch into warm, well-drained soil and firm gently. These round, stringless pods hold well in heat and resist mosaic virus. Harvest while the pods are smooth and before the beans inside bulge.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and keep beds weeded while plants establish. This bush romano bears flat, broad pods with rich, meaty flavor in a heavy set. Gather the flat pods while still flexible and before they turn fibrous.

planting.note: Sow after frost; upright plants hold pods off the soil.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil and give steady moisture through pod set. Jade produces long, slim, deep-green pods on tall, upright bushes that stay clean. Pick often for tender, straight beans that keep well after harvest.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Sow directly about an inch deep and keep the soil moist until emergence. This dependable variety sets long, slender, stringless pods high on the plant for easy picking. Harvest young for the most tender, crisp beans.

planting.note: Sow after frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and thin seedlings to stand a few inches apart. This classic all-purpose bean yields straight, round green pods in a heavy early set. Pick pods when they snap cleanly and before seeds show through.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into warmed soil.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and water steadily as pods form. Greencrop bears long, flat, deep-green pods on vigorous bushes. Harvest while pods are flat and tender rather than rounded and fully filled.

planting.note: Germinates in cooler soil than most snap beans.

directions: Sow an inch deep in spring soil, taking advantage of this heirloom's tolerance of cooler ground. The violet pods are borne on sturdy bushes and shift to green with cooking. Harvest while the pods remain smooth and crisp.

common_name: Pencil Pod Golden Wax Bush Bean

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant seeds about an inch deep and keep the bed evenly moist through germination. This heirloom wax bean yields round, bright-yellow, stringless pods with fine flavor. Pick the golden pods young while they snap crisply.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and water consistently through flowering. Slim, bright-gold pods hang cleanly on upright bushes for easy harvest. Pick the straight yellow pods while slender and tender.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil for this dual-purpose Dutch heirloom. The flat cream pods carry purple streaks that fade with cooking, and they can also be shelled if left to mature. Harvest young as a snap bean while the stripes are vivid and pods are tender.

planting.note: Sow after frost; succession sow for continuous filet beans.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and keep picking frequent to hold quality. This French filet bean produces very long, thin, slender green pods best gathered young. Harvest daily when the pods are pencil-thin for the finest, most tender texture.

planting.note: Sow after frost; compact plants suit close spacing.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil on short, tidy plants that hold pods well. Masai bears slim haricot-vert pods in heavy clusters near the top. Pick young and often for the tender, gourmet-quality beans.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warmed soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and keep the bed moist until sprouts appear. Slenderette produces smooth, round, dark-green pods that stay stringless and tender. Harvest while the slim pods snap cleanly for the best eating quality.

planting.note: An early heirloom; sow as soon as frost danger passes.

directions: Sow an inch deep once soil warms and thin to a few inches apart. This early heirloom bears flat, stringless green pods over a long season. Pick the flat pods while young and flexible for tender beans.

planting.note: Sow after frost; succession sow for a steady filet supply.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and harvest promptly to keep pods slim. Tavera is a French filet type bearing straight, slender green pods that mature evenly. Pick daily while thin for the tender, refined texture prized in this class.

planting.note: Compact habit well suited to pots and small beds.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in a container or bed of warm, rich soil. This compact award-winning variety holds slender pods above tidy foliage for clean picking. Harvest the thin green pods often to keep the plant producing.

planting.note: Bush form of the famous heirloom; sow after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and keep evenly watered as pods develop. This bush form offers the old-fashioned flavor of Kentucky Wonder without staking. Harvest the long, meaty pods while young, or leave some to shell as dry beans.

planting.note: Sow after frost; sets pods in a concentrated flush.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and thin to stand a few inches apart. Strike produces straight, round, dark-green pods in a heavy, uniform set. Harvest over a short window while pods are smooth and crisp.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and keep the bed evenly moist. Bronco bears smooth, medium-green, stringless pods on sturdy, productive plants. Pick regularly once pods reach full length but remain tender.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and firm the soil over them gently. This wax bean produces flat-to-oval, buttery-yellow pods on compact bushes. Harvest the golden pods while young and stringless.

planting.note: Sow after frost; heat-tolerant and dependable.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and water steadily through pod set. Cherokee Wax bears round, bright-yellow, stringless pods with black seeds inside. Pick the golden pods while smooth and crisp for the best flavor.

planting.note: Sow after frost; ideal for close-spaced filet rows.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil on this compact French filet variety. Nickel bears very slim, straight pods that hold their fine quality well. Harvest young and frequently while the pods are thin and tender.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil and keep the bed evenly moist. Rocdor is a French yellow filet bean bearing slim, bright-yellow, straight pods. Pick daily while thin for the tender, gourmet texture.

planting.note: Sow after frost; concentrated set favors single-harvest picking.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and water consistently through flowering. Festina sets slim, straight, medium-green pods in a heavy, uniform flush. Harvest while pods are slender and before the seeds fill out.

planting.note: Sow at the base of a sturdy trellis or pole after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep beside a strong support and guide the vines up as they climb. This vigorous old heirloom bears long, meaty pods over a long season. Keep the pods picked to hold quality, or let some dry on the vine for shelling.

planting.note: Sow at a trellis after frost; a Kentucky Wonder x Blue Lake cross.

directions: Sow an inch deep at the foot of a tall support and train the climbing vines upward. This award-winning cross combines Blue Lake flavor with Kentucky Wonder vigor. Harvest the long, straight, round pods while smooth and tender.

planting.note: Sow beside a trellis after frost for a long picking season.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at a sturdy support and let the vines climb freely. This pole strain of Blue Lake bears round, dark-green pods with classic full flavor. Pick continuously to keep new pods setting through summer.

planting.note: Heat- and drought-tolerant; sow at a trellis after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep beside a tall support in warm soil, where these vines thrive in heat. Rattlesnake bears green pods streaked with purple that fade when cooked. Harvest young as snap beans, or leave to mature for tan, speckled dry beans.

planting.note: Sow at a trellis after frost; pods stay tender even when long.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at a strong support and guide the vigorous vines upward. Fortex is a French filet pole type whose long, slim, round pods stay stringless even at full size. Harvest at any length from slender to very long for tender, flavorful beans.

planting.note: Italian flat-pod pole bean; sow at a trellis after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep at the base of a sturdy support and train the vines as they climb. This classic Italian pole bean bears broad, flat pods with rich, distinctive flavor. Harvest the flat pods while flexible and before they toughen.

planting.note: Spanish romano type; sow at a trellis after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep beside a tall support and let the vines climb. Musica produces long, wide, flat green pods that stay tender and sweet. Pick the broad pods while young for the best flavor and texture.

planting.note: Early romano pole type suited to cooler regions.

directions: Sow an inch deep at a trellis after frost, an early choice for short-season areas. Northeaster bears broad, flat romano pods earlier than most pole types. Harvest the flat pods while tender and before the seeds swell.

planting.note: French filet pole type; sow at a trellis after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at a strong support and let the vines climb. Emerite bears long, slim, round filet pods that remain stringless and tender. Pick often while thin for the fine gourmet quality this type is known for.

planting.note: Italian purple-podded pole bean; sow at a trellis after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep at a support and enjoy the lavender flowers and purple stems as vines climb. This Italian heirloom bears deep-violet pods that turn green when cooked. Harvest the slim purple pods young while tender.

common_name: Cherokee Trail of Tears Pole Bean

planting.note: Dual-purpose heirloom; sow at a trellis after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep beside a tall support and let the vigorous vines climb. This storied heirloom bears green pods with a purple tinge and glossy black seeds. Pick young for snap beans, or leave to dry on the vine for shelling.

planting.note: Southern heirloom pole bean; sow at a trellis after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep at a sturdy support and train the vines upward. McCaslan is a Southern favorite bearing long, flat, meaty pods with rich flavor. Harvest young as snap beans, or let pods mature for white shelling beans.

planting.note: European romano pole type; sow at a trellis after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at a tall support and guide the climbing vines. Helda bears long, broad, flat green pods that stay tender and full-flavored. Harvest the wide pods while flexible and before they turn fibrous.

planting.note: Yellow romano pole type; sow at a trellis after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep at a strong support and let the vines climb. Gold Marie bears flat, golden-yellow romano pods with sweet, rich flavor. Pick the broad yellow pods while young and tender.

planting.note: Heat-tolerant Southern heirloom; sow at a trellis after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at a support in warm soil, where these vines shrug off summer heat. Louisiana Purple Pod bears deep-purple pods that turn green when cooked. Harvest the purple pods young while smooth and tender.

planting.note: Sow after frost; slim filet pods held high on the plant.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and keep the bed evenly moist. Caprice bears long, slender, dark-green filet pods held conveniently near the top of the plant. Pick often while the pods are thin for the tender, refined quality.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost when the soil is reliably warm.

directions: Plant seeds one inch deep in rows and thin lightly so plants stand a hand's width apart. Pick pods young for fresh shelling beans, or leave them to fill and dry on the bush for a storage bean. Water steadily during flowering and pod set to keep the maroon-flecked seeds plump.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost danger ends and soil reaches at least sixty degrees.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil and keep weeds down while plants establish. The flame-streaked pods are best harvested at the green-shell stage when the beans inside are full but tender. For dry beans, wait until pods turn papery and the cranberry-splashed seeds harden.

planting.note: Wait for settled warm weather before sowing this heat-loving bean.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a sunny bed and space plants a few inches apart to let air move through. The half-runner habit may sprawl slightly, so give it a little room or a low trellis. Let pods dry down completely, then thresh out the small, glossy black beans and store them cool and dry.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost once soil temperatures hold above sixty degrees.

directions: Sow seeds one inch deep in full sun and avoid overwatering once pods begin to fill. This classic half-runner benefits from a dry finish to the season so the mottled beans cure cleanly. Pull the plants when most pods are tan and papery, then shell and store the speckled seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow into warmed soil after the last frost date.

directions: Plant seeds one inch deep and firm the soil to hold moisture around them. The compact bushes carry many small pods that mature nearly all at once for easy harvest. Let the pods dry fully on the plant, then thresh out the little white beans and store them dry.

planting.note: Sow after frost has passed and give this bean a long warm season.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in full sun once soil is thoroughly warm. Kidney beans need most of the summer to fill and dry, so plant early in the warm window. Harvest when pods turn brown and papery, then shell out the large dark red seeds and cure before storage.

planting.note: Grown as an annual; sow after frost, though roots may overwinter in mild zones.

directions: Sow seeds one to two inches deep at the base of a sturdy trellis, teepee, or fence. The vigorous vines carry brilliant scarlet flowers that draw hummingbirds and set long pods. Pick young pods as snap beans, or leave them to mature and dry for the large purple-and-black shell beans.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost against a tall support in a sunny spot.

directions: Plant seeds an inch or two deep beside a trellis and keep the soil evenly moist through flowering. This old runner bears bicolor red-and-white blossoms followed by long tender pods. Let the pods mature and dry on the vine to collect the large mottled seeds for shelling.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost at the base of a tall trellis.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and provide sturdy poles or netting for the climbing vines. This productive heirloom yields heavily and holds its beans well in the pod. Harvest once pods dry and rattle, then shell the plump maroon-and-white swirled seeds.

planting.note: Sow directly once soil warms after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant seeds one inch deep in warm soil and keep the bed moist during germination. The purple-streaked yellow pods are excellent as snap beans when young. Left longer, the pods fill for shelling or dry down to reveal fawn-colored seeds.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil once the frost risk has ended.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. This New England heirloom is notably drought-tolerant and crops well even in leaner soils. Harvest the beans when pods are dry, revealing the reddish soldier-shaped marking near the eye.

planting.note: Direct sow a week or two after the last frost.

directions: Sow seeds one inch deep and keep the soil evenly moist until emergence. A traditional baking bean, it produces creamy white seeds each marked with a golden eye. Let pods dry on the bush, then shell and store the cured beans.

planting.note: Sow directly once frost danger passes and soil is warm.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep in full sun and keep weeds in check early on. This hardy northern baking bean produces round tan seeds on sturdy bushes. Harvest when pods are dry and brittle, then shell and cure the beans.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and space plants a few inches apart in the row. This thin-skinned heirloom cooks quickly and holds its rich golden-and-maroon markings. Harvest when the pods dry down, then shell the striking swirled seeds.

planting.note: Sow after frost at the base of a tall support.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep beside poles or a trellis for the twining vines to climb. This heat- and drought-tolerant pole bean gives snap beans from purple-streaked pods and dries to buff seeds with brown speckles. Pick young for snaps, or leave pods to dry fully for shell and storage beans.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost beside a sturdy climbing support.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep at the foot of a trellis and let the vigorous vines climb. This storied heirloom produces purple-tinged pods and shiny black dry beans. Harvest young pods as snaps or allow them to dry on the vine for the black seeds.

planting.note: Sow after frost where the semi-runner vines can find support.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and give the half-runner vines a low trellis or brush to climb. This Northern Plains heirloom is well adapted to short, cool-summer seasons. Let pods dry fully, then shell out the white seeds marked with a dark shield pattern.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in warmed soil.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and thin plants to a few inches apart. This drought-hardy Plains heirloom yields dependably even in dry seasons. Harvest when pods dry down, then shell the pale golden-yellow seeds.

planting.note: Sow after frost and give the semi-vining plants some support.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and let the half-runner vines scramble on brush or a low trellis. This Northern Plains bean sets well in cooler summers and stores beautifully. Let pods dry on the plant, then shell out the small reddish-brown seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and keep the bed evenly moist while plants establish. This Scandinavian-American heirloom holds its shape and skin when cooked. Harvest when pods are dry, then shell and cure the small tan-brown beans.

planting.note: Sow directly once soil warms after the last frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and firm the soil to hold moisture. This classic French shell bean is harvested slightly immature to keep its pale green color. Pick pods at the mature-green stage for tender flageolets, or dry them fully for storage.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost beside a tall trellis or poles.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep at the base of sturdy supports for the tall vines. This old dual-purpose pole bean gives stringless snaps and white dry beans. Harvest young pods for snaps, or leave them to dry on the vine for shelling.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and space plants a few inches apart. This productive bush bean thickens soups nicely and dries down reliably. Harvest when the pods are brittle, then shell the plump tan seeds.

planting.note: Sow after frost once soil temperatures are reliably warm.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in full sun and keep the row weeded early. This bush bean can be used as a green shell bean or matured to round black dry beans. Harvest when the pods dry down, then shell and cure the beans.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warmed soil.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and keep the soil moist until seedlings appear. Prized for soups, this heirloom breaks down into a creamy broth when cooked. Let pods dry fully, then shell the pale greenish-yellow seeds.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in full sun and avoid overwatering as pods fill. This Southwestern heirloom tolerates heat and dry conditions well. Harvest when pods are papery, then shell the large flat white seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warmed soil.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and thin to a few inches apart. This Montana heirloom yields small golden beans on early, compact bushes. Harvest when pods dry down, then shell and store the cured beans.

planting.note: Sow after frost once soil is warm.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and give the half-runner plants a little room. Use young pods as snap beans or mature the seeds for a dry bean. Harvest dried pods to reveal small seeds patterned in tan and dark brown.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and keep the bed weed-free while plants establish. This improved yellow-eye baking bean produces heavily on sturdy bushes. Harvest when pods are dry, then shell the white seeds with a golden eye.

planting.note: Sow after frost beside a trellis for the climbing vines.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at the base of poles or netting. This old climbing bean works as a snap when young and dries to white seeds splashed with maroon. Let the pods dry on the vine, then shell the two-tone seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and thin to a few inches apart. This dependable bush bean matures early and dries down evenly. Harvest when pods are brittle, then shell the tan seeds streaked with brown.

planting.note: Sow after frost beside a sturdy climbing support.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and train the vines onto poles or a trellis. The plump pods are best shelled green when the seeds are full and tender. Left to dry, they yield buff seeds heavily splashed with cranberry red.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost at the base of a trellis.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and give the climbing vines strong support. This Italian borlotto is grown mainly for its plump green-shell beans. Harvest pods when the red-streaked seeds are full, or dry them fully for storage.

planting.note: Sow after frost where the half-runner vines can climb.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and provide brush or a low trellis for support. This Southern heirloom is popular as a fall shell bean, hence the October name. Shell the plump pinkish-tan seeds green, or let the pods dry for a storage bean.

planting.note: Sow after frost and allow a long warm season to mature.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil and keep weeds down early. Kidney beans need a full summer to fill their large pods, so sow promptly. Harvest when pods are brown and dry, then shell the pink-red kidney-shaped seeds.

planting.note: Sow after frost beside poles for the climbing vines.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and let the vines climb a sturdy trellis. This Appalachian greasy bean has smooth, hairless pods packed tightly with seeds. Use as a snap when young, or dry the pods to shell out the crowded seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and thin to a few inches apart in the row. This old dual-purpose bush bean gives flat snap pods and red kidney-type dry beans. Harvest young pods for snaps, or leave them to dry for the deep red seeds.

planting.note: Sow after frost beside a trellis in full sun.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and train the climbing vines up strong supports. This renowned Italian borlotto is grown for its large, creamy shell beans. Shell the red-streaked seeds when the pods are full, or dry them for winter storage.

planting.note: Sow as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring, well before the last frost.

directions: Sow seed about one inch deep in a single or double row and keep the soil evenly moist until sprouts emerge. Provide a short trellis or netting for the slender vines to climb and to keep the long pods off the ground. Pick when the pods are plump and rounded but still bright green, before the peas inside toughen.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool early-spring soil for a June harvest.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep in loosened, well-drained soil once frost danger has mostly passed. The compact bushy vines need little or no support but appreciate a few twiggy branches to lean on. Harvest the blunt, tightly filled pods when they feel firm and full for the sweetest flavor.

planting.note: Tolerant of both cold and heat, so it can be sown a little later than most peas.

directions: Sow one inch deep in early spring, and because this variety holds up in warm weather you can also try a late-summer planting for fall. Keep plants watered during any hot spells to preserve pod quality. Pick the rounded pods promptly once they fill out, since regular harvesting keeps the vines productive.

planting.note: Sow into workable spring soil for a heavy late-spring crop.

directions: Set seed an inch deep in fertile soil and keep rows weed-free while the vines establish. This heirloom, also sold as Homesteader, packs eight or nine sweet peas into each curved pod and tolerates warmth better than many old varieties. Shell soon after picking, as the sugars fade quickly once the pods leave the plant.

planting.note: An early sort for cold soil; plant as soon as the ground thaws.

directions: Sow one inch deep in early spring and give the medium-tall vines a trellis or pea netting to climb. The blunt dark-green pods hold their quality on the vine longer than most, so harvesting can be a little forgiving. Pick when pods are well filled but the peas are still tender and sweet.

planting.note: A tall late heirloom; sow early and plan for sturdy support.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep alongside a tall trellis, fence, or netting at least five feet high, since these vigorous vines climb well over head height. Keep them evenly watered so the large pods fill completely. Harvest the long pods when they are packed with big, sweet peas over an extended picking season.

planting.note: An early dwarf that fits small gardens; sow in cool spring soil.

directions: Plant one inch deep in early spring; the short vines are self-supporting but do well with a low twiggy support. Despite the compact plants, the dark-green pods are large and well filled with sweet peas. Begin picking as soon as pods plump up to encourage continued set.

planting.note: Cold-tolerant and good for freezing; sow as soon as soil is workable.

directions: Sow an inch deep in early spring, spacing seeds a couple of inches apart in the row. The vines set pods in pairs near the top, which makes gathering fast and efficient. Harvest when the pods are full and glossy; the peas hold their quality well for freezing.

planting.note: Disease-resistant and productive; sow in cool early-spring soil.

directions: Direct sow one inch deep as soon as the ground can be worked, and offer a low net for the semi-dwarf vines. This variety resists common pea viruses and powdery mildew, giving reliable yields. Pick the slender well-filled pods, which often carry ten or more peas each, while they are still bright and tender.

planting.note: Heavy-cropping British maincrop; sow spring for summer harvest.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a flat drill, spacing seed a couple of inches apart. The pods form in pairs at the top of the plant, and the heavy tendril growth helps the vines cling to netting. Harvest the long, tightly filled pods regularly to keep the plants cropping over several weeks.

planting.note: An early dwarf suited to successional sowings from spring onward.

directions: Sow one inch deep and make repeat sowings every few weeks for a long picking season. The short vines need only twiggy sticks or a low net for support. Pick the pointed, well-filled pods young and often, as this variety responds to frequent harvesting with continued set.

planting.note: Hardy round-seeded type that can be autumn or very early spring sown.

directions: Sow one inch deep in late autumn or as early as the soil permits in spring, since the round smooth seed withstands cold and wet better than wrinkled types. The very short vines make it a good choice for exposed or windy plots. Harvest the compact pods in late spring for one of the earliest crops of the season.

planting.note: A reliable maincrop; sow in spring for a heavy summer harvest.

directions: Sow an inch deep in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and support the vines with netting. This popular variety resists downy mildew and produces blunt pods carried in pairs. Pick when the pods are dark green and packed with peas for the best yield and flavor.

planting.note: A tiny heirloom ideal for containers and window boxes.

directions: Sow one inch deep in a pot or garden bed; the miniature plants need no staking at all. Despite their small stature they set full-sized pods and tolerate cold well, making them good for early or container crops. Harvest the little pods once they round out, and pick often to keep the compact plants producing.

planting.note: Known for oversized pods; sow in cool spring soil.

directions: Direct sow one inch deep and give the vines a net to climb as they can reach three feet. The extra-large pods hold up to ten big peas each and are easy to shell. Pick when the pods are full and glossy for a generous, sweet harvest.

planting.note: A dual-purpose heirloom grown for young peas or dried soup peas.

directions: Sow an inch deep next to a tall support, as the bicolor-flowered vines climb vigorously. Eat the peas fresh while young or let the striking purple-blue pods dry fully on the plant for winter soups. For dried peas, harvest once the pods rattle and the seeds are hard.

planting.note: A tall Victorian heirloom needing a full-height trellis.

directions: Sow one inch deep at the base of a tall, sturdy trellis, since these old-fashioned vines can top six feet. Keep them well watered through pod fill for the sweet, wrinkled peas this heirloom is prized for. Harvest over a long season as the pods reach the row's upper reaches.

planting.note: The original tall snap pea; sow early and plan for a tall trellis.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep beside a trellis at least five or six feet tall to carry the vigorous vines. The thick, rounded pods are eaten whole, pod and all, and stay crisp and sweet. Snap them off when the pods have swelled but are still bright green and tender.

planting.note: An early dwarf snap pea, no trellis required.

directions: Sow one inch deep in cool spring soil; the compact self-supporting vines are perfect for small beds and containers. This early variety produces thick, edible-pod snaps well ahead of the tall types. Pick the plump, sweet pods whole while they snap crisply.

planting.note: A compact, disease-resistant snap pea; sow in early spring.

directions: Sow an inch deep and give the medium-height vines a low trellis or net. This variety resists powdery mildew and pea enation virus, producing dark-green, thick-walled pods. Harvest the short, blocky pods whole when they are full and crunchy.

planting.note: An improved, mildew-resistant version of the classic Sugar Snap.

directions: Sow one inch deep at the foot of a tall trellis to support the strong vines. This selection matures a bit earlier than the original and resists powdery mildew for a longer season. Pick the fat, edible pods whole when they are crisp and full of sugar.

planting.note: A stringless snap pea on compact vines; sow in cool soil.

directions: Sow one inch deep and give the medium vines a short net for support. The nearly stringless pods need no de-stringing, making them quick to prepare. Pick the sweet, crisp pods whole as soon as they plump up, and harvest often to keep them coming.

planting.note: A stringless bush snap pea needing no support.

directions: Sow one inch deep in cool spring soil; the self-supporting bush vines are tidy and trellis-free. This was one of the first truly stringless snap peas, so the pods are ready to eat straight off the plant. Harvest the crisp pods whole once they fill out.

planting.note: A semi-leafless snap pea whose tendrils help it self-support.

directions: Sow an inch deep and set plants close so the abundant tendrils can knit together and hold the vines upright with little staking. The semi-leafless habit improves air flow and makes the pods easy to spot. Pick the sweet, stringless pods whole when they are crisp and rounded.

planting.note: A tall heirloom snap pea; sow early with a tall trellis.

directions: Sow one inch deep beside a tall support for the vigorous heirloom vines. This early Amish family variety bears slender, tender-crisp pods that are sweet eaten whole. Pick the pods young while still bright green for the best snap and flavor.

planting.note: A hyper-tendril snap pea with ornamental bicolor flowers.

directions: Sow one inch deep along a tall trellis; the profuse tendrils let the vines grip supports and each other firmly. The pretty two-tone purple-and-white blossoms give way to sweet, edible snap pods, and the shoots and tendrils are also good in salads. Harvest the pods whole when plump, and pick tendrils young for greens.

common_name: Oregon Sugar Pod II Snow Pea

planting.note: A widely grown, disease-resistant snow pea; sow in early spring.

directions: Sow one inch deep and give the compact vines a low net or brush support. This dependable variety resists several pea diseases and bears large, flat, edible pods in pairs. Pick the pods while they are still flat and tender, before the peas inside begin to swell.

planting.note: A large-podded snow pea; sow in cool spring soil.

directions: Sow an inch deep and support the medium vines with netting. The extra-large flat pods stay sweet and tender even at larger sizes, and the variety resists common pea diseases. Harvest while the pods are flat and crisp, or let them fill slightly and eat like a snap pea.

planting.note: A tall heirloom snow pea; sow early with a tall trellis.

directions: Sow one inch deep beside a tall trellis to carry the vigorous vines. The large flat pods are tender and sweet, and the plants tolerate some heat and resist fusarium wilt. Pick the pods while flat and bright green, before the peas develop, for the best texture.

planting.note: An early dwarf snow pea for small gardens; little support needed.

directions: Sow one inch deep in cool spring soil; the short, sturdy vines are nearly self-supporting. This early variety bears its flat pods in clusters of two or three, making picking quick. Harvest while the pods are flat and tender for stir-fries and salads.

planting.note: An old edible-pod heirloom also grown for pea shoots.

directions: Sow an inch deep in cool soil; the vines carry attractive purple blossoms and moderate height. Grow it for tender flat snow-pea pods or sow thickly and cut the young shoots as a mild pea green. Pick the pods while flat and stringless, before the peas swell inside.

planting.note: A yellow-podded heirloom snow pea with purple flowers.

directions: Sow one inch deep at the base of a tall trellis for the vigorous purple-flowered vines. The lemon-yellow flat pods are striking in the garden and on the plate, best eaten young. Harvest while the pods are flat and tender, and pick regularly to keep the vines flowering.

planting.note: A productive large-podded snow pea; sow in early spring.

directions: Sow an inch deep and give the medium vines a net to climb. This variety produces heavy yields of broad, flat pods that stay tender and sweet. Pick the pods while flat and bright green before the peas begin to fill.

planting.note: A very tall snow pea also grown for its edible shoots.

directions: Sow one inch deep next to a tall, strong trellis, as these vines can reach seven feet or more. The huge flat pods stay tender even when large, and the abundant shoots make excellent pea greens. Harvest the pods while flat and pick young tendrils for eating.

planting.note: A French heirloom snow pea with purple blossoms and large pods.

directions: Sow one inch deep beside a tall trellis for the vigorous, purple-flowered vines. This Provençal heirloom bears very large, sweet flat pods that remain tender at good size. Harvest while the pods are flat and glossy, before the peas swell within.

planting.note: Sow once soil is thoroughly warm, one to two weeks after the last spring frost.

directions: Push seeds about one inch deep into warm, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. This bush type tolerates heat and poor soils, so avoid heavy nitrogen that pushes leaves over pods. Pick pods for shelling when they plump and the seed eyes darken, or leave them to dry on the plant for storage peas.

planting.note: Plant in late spring or early summer once soil warms past 65 degrees.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep in loosened soil and water them in well. This disease-tolerant crowder holds up in Southern heat and produces silvery pods packed with tightly crowded peas. Shell when pods turn from green to a pale silver-tan for the best fresh flavor.

planting.note: Sow after the soil is warm; this heirloom thrives in long, hot summers.

directions: Sow the speckled seeds one inch deep in warm ground and give the semi-vining plants a little room to sprawl. This tough old field pea fixes nitrogen well and doubles as a soil-building cover. Harvest dry pods once they turn brown and brittle for buff-and-brown speckled peas.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil once nights stay reliably mild.

directions: Plant seeds one inch deep and thin to give each bushy plant good airflow. The name comes from pods that open easily along a seam, making shelling of the pale cream peas quick. Pick when pods swell and lose their green cast for tender fresh cream peas.

planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm and settled.

directions: Drop seeds an inch deep in fertile, warm soil and keep the bed weed-free while plants establish. This variety is prized for unusually large, crowded peas that shell out generously. Harvest at the green-shell stage when pods are full but still soft.

planting.note: Sow into hot soil; these vines need warmth and a long growing season.

directions: Sow seeds one inch deep at the base of a tall trellis and let the vigorous vines climb. The deep burgundy pods can reach well over a foot long and hold their color best when picked young. Harvest while pods are pencil-thin and pliable, before the seeds bulge.

planting.note: Sow only after soil is warm; cold, wet ground rots lima seed.

directions: Sow seeds one to one-and-a-half inches deep in warm, well-drained soil, eye-side down. This compact baby lima needs no support and bears small, tender pale-green seeds. Pick pods when they feel plump and the beans inside round out.

planting.note: Wait until soil holds steady warmth before sowing this heat-tolerant bush lima.

directions: Plant seeds about an inch deep and keep soil evenly moist without waterlogging. This dependable bush type sets thick pods of large, buttery beans even in summer heat. Harvest when the pods bulge with three or four fat seeds but are still bright green.

common_name: King of the Garden Pole Lima Bean

planting.note: Sow at the base of a tall pole once the soil is reliably warm.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep beside a strong trellis or pole for the vigorous climbing vines. This heirloom pole lima bears large flat beans over a long season if kept picked. Harvest the broad pods when they swell with big, flat seeds but remain green.

planting.note: Sow once frost is past and soil has warmed; this bush type is drought-hardy.

directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep in warm soil and thin plants to give each room. This tough Southern heirloom bears small buff seeds speckled with purple and shrugs off heat and dry spells. Pick young for fresh butterbeans or let pods dry for a keeping crop.

planting.note: Plant in warm soil after frost; well suited to hot, humid summers.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and keep the bed moist until the sturdy bushes establish. These small round butterpeas are speckled and rich, holding up in Deep South heat. Harvest when pods fill with several small, plump seeds.

planting.note: Sow in very early spring, or in fall for mild-winter areas, since favas love cool weather.

directions: Push the large seeds two inches deep into cool, workable soil and space rows for airflow. The upright stalks may need light staking once the heavy pods set. Harvest when pods are plump and glossy, then shell out the big flat beans and slip off their skins.

planting.note: An excellent fall-sown fava for overwintering in mild regions.

directions: Sow seeds two inches deep in autumn where winters are mild, or as early in spring as the ground can be worked. This cold-hardy Spanish type overwinters well and produces long pods of pale green beans. Pick pods young and tender for the sweetest flavor.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost when soil is warm; this is one of the earlier edamame types.

directions: Sow seeds one inch deep in warm soil and thin to a couple of inches apart. Plants set clusters of fuzzy green pods that fill quickly in warm weather. Harvest the whole plant when most pods are plump but still bright green, then steam and pop the beans free.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil once frost danger has fully passed.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and keep them consistently moist through pod fill. This variety produces large, well-filled pods with two to three big beans each. Pick when pods are bright green and swollen, before any yellowing begins.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil after frost; a productive standard edamame.

directions: Sow seeds one inch deep in a sunny bed and thin lightly once up. Sturdy plants hold their pods well off the ground and fill evenly. Harvest the entire plant when pods are plump and green, then boil briefly in salted water.

planting.note: Sow raw shelled nuts after frost into loose, warm, sandy soil.

directions: Plant raw shelled kernels about one to two inches deep in light, loose soil. After the yellow flowers fade, the stalks bend down and push pegs into the ground where the nuts develop, so keep soil loose and mounded. Dig the whole plant when foliage yellows in fall and cure the pods in a dry, airy spot.

planting.note: Sow in early spring; chickpeas prefer a cool start and dislike transplanting.

directions: Sow seeds about one-and-a-half inches deep in well-drained soil and avoid overhead watering. The ferny plants each carry small pods holding one or two round peas. Let pods dry and turn tan on the plant, then thresh out the beige seeds for storage.

planting.note: Sow after frost at the base of a tall trellis; loves heat.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep beside a strong support and let the fast vines climb through summer. Purple stems, pink blooms, and glossy magenta pods make this both ornamental and edible. Eat only young pods and thoroughly cooked seeds, since raw mature beans are not safe to eat.

planting.note: Sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring for a cool-season crop.

directions: Sow seeds one inch deep in cool, workable soil and provide light support for the vines. This reliable shelling pea packs long slim pods with seven to nine sweet peas each. Pick pods when they fill out and feel firm but before they harden and fade.

planting.note: Sow in early spring in cool soil for a long-season pulse crop.

directions: Sow the large seeds about an inch deep in well-drained soil and keep weeds down early. Tall spikes of white flowers give way to pods holding flat cream-colored seeds. Harvest dried seed in late season, then soak and repeatedly rinse the beans to leach bitterness before eating.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost for a full-season dry crop.

directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and keep evenly moist through flowering. The low, somewhat hairy plants set clusters of small pods that ripen in stages. Gather pods as they blacken and dry, then shell out the small black seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow after the soil warms in late spring.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a sunny bed with deep, moisture-holding soil, since this variety roots strongly and benefits from a steady water supply. Mulch to conserve soil moisture and reduce stress in dry weather. Harvest the slender dark fruits young for the best texture, before the skin toughens.

planting.note: Sow directly once the soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in fertile soil and give this Italian heirloom plenty of room to spread. Water consistently and side-dress with compost as the plants bulk up. Pick the ribbed, dark-and-light striped fruits at six to eight inches, when the flesh is still firm and sweet.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep and allow generous spacing, as this ribbed Italian heirloom grows into a large, sprawling plant. It is known for rich flavor but a modest yield, so pick often to encourage more fruit. Harvest the strongly ridged, gray-flecked squash young; the male blossoms are also prized for stuffing.

planting.note: Sow after frost danger in warm ground.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm soil and keep well watered during fruit set. This compact, productive variety handles warm regions well and bears heavily. Harvest the pale gray-green, faintly mottled fruits at six to eight inches for the finest texture.

planting.note: Direct sow when the soil is warm and settled.

directions: Sow an inch deep in rich soil and keep the plants evenly moist. This variety produces round, ball-shaped dark green fruits that are ideal for stuffing. Pick them at two to three inches across for tender skin, harvesting frequently to keep new fruit coming.

planting.note: Plant directly in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep and give this French heirloom room to bush out. The round, pale green fruits are traditionally hollowed and stuffed. Harvest them young at golf-ball to tennis-ball size for the most delicate flavor and thin skin.

planting.note: Sow directly once frost has passed and soil is warm.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a warm, sunny bed with fertile soil. This reliable hybrid forms an open plant that yields uniform, medium-green fruit over a long window. Cut the squash at seven to eight inches and pick every couple of days at peak.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after all frost risk ends.

directions: Sow an inch deep and keep evenly watered as the plants establish. As the name suggests, the nearly spineless stems and leaf stalks make harvesting far more comfortable. Cut the medium-green fruits at six to eight inches and pick often to keep production high.

planting.note: Sow after frost; sets fruit even without pollinators.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil. This parthenocarpic hybrid sets dark green fruit without pollination, making it useful under row covers or in cool, low-bee conditions. Harvest at six to eight inches for smooth, seedless-textured squash.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil once frost has passed.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a sunny, fertile bed and thin to strong plants. This award-winning hybrid bears distinctive mottled light-and-dark green fruit over a long season. Pick the marbled squash young at five to seven inches for peak tenderness and flavor.

planting.note: Suited to containers; sow after the soil warms.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a large pot or bed, as this French variety was bred for compact, container-friendly growth. The upright, open habit keeps fruit visible and easy to reach. Harvest the medium-green squash at six to eight inches and water containers regularly.

planting.note: Direct sow when soil is reliably warm.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and keep the soil warm and moist for quick germination. This early hybrid produces glossy dark green fruit on an open plant that is easy to pick. Harvest at six to eight inches, checking daily during warm weather when fruit sizes up fast.

planting.note: Direct sow once frost has passed and soil warms.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm soil and keep evenly watered through the harvest season. This old favorite forms bumpy yellow fruit with a curved, tapered neck. Pick when the squash are four to six inches long, before the skin hardens and the bumps deepen.

planting.note: Sow directly in warm soil in late spring.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and give the bushy plants full sun and steady moisture. The deep golden, curve-necked fruits have rich flavor and firm flesh. Harvest young at five to six inches and pick regularly so the plant keeps setting new fruit.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm, rich soil and keep the bed evenly moist. This straightneck hybrid carries a virus-resistance trait that keeps the fruit clean and bright yellow. Harvest the smooth squash at five to six inches for tender, blemish-free eating.

planting.note: Sow directly once the soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Sow an inch deep in fertile soil and keep well watered as fruit forms. This distinctive hybrid bears slender fruit that is yellow on top with a pale green blossom end. Pick at four to six inches for firm, nutty-flavored squash and harvest frequently.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil and thin to sturdy bush plants. This award-winning scallop squash forms bright golden, saucer-shaped fruit with scalloped edges. Harvest at two to three inches across for baby squash or up to four inches for slicing, picking often.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost when soil warms.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and give the compact bushes full sun. This old heirloom produces pale, greenish-white scalloped fruit shaped like a flying saucer. Pick at three to four inches across while the skin is still soft and the flesh sweet.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and keep the bed evenly moist during warm weather. This heirloom scallop bears flattened, pale green fruit with a gently ribbed, saucer shape. Harvest small at two to three inches for the most tender texture and mild flavor.

planting.note: Sow directly once soil is warm in late spring.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm soil and thin to strong bush plants. This award-winning hybrid produces light green scalloped fruit early and steadily. Harvest at two to four inches across, cutting often to keep the compact plants productive.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when the ground warms.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil and keep evenly watered. This early hybrid scallop bears bright yellow, saucer-shaped fruit on a tidy plant. Pick at one to two inches for baby squash or up to three inches for slicing, harvesting frequently.

planting.note: Sow directly in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and keep the soil warm and moist for even germination. This award-winning variety bears pale gray-green fruit lightly striped in darker green. Harvest at six to eight inches for tender, flavorful squash and pick often to sustain the crop.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost has passed.

directions: Sow an inch deep in fertile, warm soil and keep the plants well watered. This Middle Eastern cousa-type squash forms pale green, tapered fruit with a bulbous end and nutty flavor. Harvest at three to five inches while tender, picking daily during the peak season.

planting.note: Sow directly once the soil has thoroughly warmed.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil and provide steady moisture. This cousa-type squash bears pale green, faintly speckled fruit that tapers toward the stem. Harvest young at four to six inches for delicate flesh well suited to stuffing and stews.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and keep the plants evenly moist. This golden hybrid zucchini holds a bright, uniform yellow color and resists common squash viruses. Harvest the straight fruit at six to eight inches, cutting frequently for continued production.

planting.note: Sow directly in warm soil once frost risk ends.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and keep the bed warm and moist for fast emergence. This early hybrid produces medium-green, lightly speckled fruit on an open, easy-to-pick plant. Harvest at six to eight inches and check daily during warm spells when fruit sizes quickly.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and thin to strong seedlings. This hybrid combines nearly spineless stems with disease resistance and a heavy set of medium-green fruit. Harvest at six to eight inches, picking often to keep the plants bearing.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost in warm ground.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a sunny, fertile bed and keep evenly watered. This dependable variety forms an upright bush with slender, medium-green fruit. Harvest at six to eight inches while the skin is tender and pick regularly through the season.

planting.note: Direct sow when soil is warm and settled.

directions: Sow an inch deep in rich, warm soil and keep the plants evenly moist. This hybrid bears round, pale green fruit that is well suited to hollowing and stuffing. Harvest at two to three inches across for tender skin, picking often to keep new fruit forming.

planting.note: Sow directly in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and keep the soil warm and evenly moist. This early straightneck hybrid produces smooth, bright yellow fruit on a compact bush. Harvest at four to six inches while the skin is glossy and soft, cutting frequently for a steady supply.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil once frost has passed.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and thin to strong plants. This hybrid carries powdery mildew resistance that helps it keep producing later into the season. Harvest the bright yellow straight fruit at four to six inches for tender, clean squash.

planting.note: Sow after frost; sets fruit even with few pollinators.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm soil and keep evenly watered as fruit sets. This parthenocarpic hybrid produces glossy medium-green fruit without pollination, making it reliable under row covers. Harvest at six to eight inches for smooth, seedless-textured squash and pick often.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and keep the plants well watered. This golden hybrid zucchini holds a rich, even yellow color on an open, easy-to-harvest plant. Cut the straight fruit at six to eight inches and pick frequently to keep it producing.

planting.note: Sow directly once the soil warms in late spring.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil and thin to sturdy bush plants. This scallop hybrid bears bright yellow, saucer-shaped fruit with a pale green blossom end. Harvest at two to three inches for baby squash, picking often to keep the plants bearing.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm ground.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and thin to the strongest plants. This early hybrid produces glossy medium-green fruit on an open plant that makes picking easy. Harvest at six to eight inches, checking daily during warm weather for best quality.

planting.note: Sow directly in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and keep the soil warm and evenly moist. This dark green hybrid holds strong color and yields heavily on an open, spineless-stemmed plant. Harvest the straight fruit at six to eight inches and pick frequently to keep production steady.

planting.note: Sow once the soil has warmed and all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Sow seeds about one inch deep in warm soil, thinning to the strongest plants once seedlings have a couple of true leaves. Give the long vines plenty of room and keep them evenly watered while fruit is sizing up. Harvest when the tan skin is hard and dull and the stem has corked; cure in a warm spot for a week or two before storing.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil after frost danger has fully passed.

directions: Sow one inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil and thin to the strongest seedlings in each hill. The tapered dark-green fruits keep exceptionally well when the rind is fully hardened. Harvest before hard frost, leaving a length of stem attached, and cure in a warm airy place.

planting.note: Direct sow once soil temperatures are dependably warm.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in a sunny bed enriched with compost. These squat, dark-green fruits with a button on the blossom end have dense, sweet orange flesh prized for baking. Harvest when the rind is hard and the vine near the fruit begins to dry, then cure before storing.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost when the ground has warmed.

directions: Plant seeds one inch deep and keep soil consistently moist during flowering and fruit set. The teardrop-shaped, orange-red fruits have smooth chestnut-flavored flesh and thin, edible skin. Harvest when the rind hardens and colors fully, leaving a stub of stem on each fruit.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in rich soil and provide steady moisture as fruit develops. This bright scarlet kabocha has sweet, deep-orange flesh with a smooth texture when cooked. Harvest when the rind turns fully red and hard, then cure briefly to concentrate the sweetness.

planting.note: Sow when nights are warm and frost is past.

directions: Plant seeds one inch deep in full sun and keep the bed weed-free while vines run. The dark-green flattened fruits have dense, sweet orange flesh reminiscent of chestnut. Harvest once the rind is hard and dull, leaving the stem attached, and cure before storage.

planting.note: Sow directly once the soil is warm after the last frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep and let the long vines sprawl across enriched, well-drained ground. This Italian heirloom bears deeply warted blue-green turban-shaped fruits with sweet orange flesh. Harvest when the rind fully hardens, then cure and store for winter cooking.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant seeds one inch deep in full sun and feed the vines with compost as they run. This Australian heirloom produces flattened, ribbed slate-blue pumpkins with fine-grained sweet flesh. Harvest when the rind is firm and the stem corks, then cure for long storage.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and allow the vigorous vines ample room to spread. This old Australian variety bears deeply ribbed blue-gray pumpkins with thick, sweet orange flesh. Harvest when fully colored and hard-skinned, then cure before storing over winter.

planting.note: Direct sow when the soil has warmed after the last frost.

directions: Plant one inch deep in rich soil and keep vines watered through the summer. This French heirloom, sometimes called the Cinderella pumpkin, forms flattened, deeply lobed scarlet fruits. Harvest when the rind is hard and vividly colored, then cure for eating or display.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm, fertile ground.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and give the vines plenty of room to sprawl. This heirloom is grown mostly for its striking turban-shaped, multicolored fruit, though the flesh is edible. Harvest when fully colored and firm, leaving a stub of stem, and cure for autumn display.

planting.note: Direct sow once soil is warm and frost is past.

directions: Plant an inch deep in full sun and keep vines evenly watered as fruit fills out. This French heirloom develops salmon-colored fruit covered in peanut-like warts, with sweet flesh good for soups. Harvest when the rind hardens, then cure before storage.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow seeds one inch deep and thin to the strongest vines in each hill. This variety bears pear-shaped fruit splashed orange and green, with sweet, fine-textured flesh. Harvest when the rind is hard and fully colored, then cure in a warm spot.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when the ground has warmed.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in rich, well-drained soil and let the vines run. This old American heirloom produces large teardrop-shaped orange fruit with sweet, deep-orange flesh once used for pies and canning. Harvest when the rind hardens, then cure and store.

planting.note: Sow after frost; compact plants suit smaller gardens.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in a warm, sunny spot; this bush-type plant stays compact enough for small beds. It produces small, ribbed orange single-serving fruits with dry, sweet flesh. Harvest when fully orange and hard-skinned, then cure briefly before use.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and keep vines watered as fruit develops. This variety bears smooth ghost-white pumpkins with bright orange flesh suitable for carving or cooking. Harvest when the rind is hard and the skin has whitened fully, leaving a length of stem attached.

planting.note: Start indoors a couple of weeks early for the long season these giants need.

directions: Start seeds indoors in warm conditions and transplant carefully once frost has passed and vines have room to sprawl enormously. For record fruit, remove all but one developing pumpkin per plant and feed and water generously. Harvest when the rind hardens, handling the massive fruit with great care.

planting.note: Sow after frost; allow a long, warm season for large fruit.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in rich soil and give the sprawling vines ample space. Limit each plant to one or two fruits and feed heavily to grow large orange-pink pumpkins. Harvest when the rind is hard and fully colored, leaving a sturdy stem attached.

planting.note: Direct sow once soil warms after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and allow the long vines room to ramble. This Australian heirloom bears large tan-orange fruit with smooth, sweet flesh good for baking. Harvest when the rind is hard and the color deepens, then cure for storage.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; needs a long warm season.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in full sun and give the long vines plenty of room. This French heirloom, sometimes sold as Fairytale, bears deeply ribbed flattened fruit that ripens from green to rich tan. Harvest when the rind hardens and colors fully, then cure for long storage.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and feed vines with compost through the season. This tan, elongated pumpkin is the classic canning type valued for smooth, sweet flesh. Harvest when the rind is hard and fully tan, then cure before processing or storing.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep and give vines room to sprawl. This old northeastern heirloom bears curved-necked tan fruit with dense, sweet flesh that keeps well. Harvest when the rind is hard and fully tan, then cure in a warm place before storage.

planting.note: Sow after frost; earlier maturity suits shorter seasons.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil; the semi-bush habit needs less room than standard butternuts. It ripens tan, bell-shaped fruit weeks earlier, making it a good choice for cool climates. Harvest when the skin is uniformly tan and hard, then cure before storing.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in a sunny, well-drained bed and thin to the strongest vines. This small sugar pumpkin has sweet, fine-grained flesh that is the classic choice for pies. Harvest when the rind turns deep orange and hardens, leaving a stub of stem on each fruit.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after all frost has passed.

directions: Sow a few seeds per hill about an inch deep and thin to two vines. This old American field pumpkin makes large, ribbed orange fruit good for carving and stock feed. Harvest when the rind is fully orange and hard, leaving a length of stem attached.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; needs a full warm season.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in rich soil and let vines spread widely. This is the classic large jack-o'-lantern pumpkin with sturdy handles and deep orange ribbed rind. Harvest when fully colored and hard-skinned, cutting the stem cleanly to keep the handle intact.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost; time planting for a fall harvest.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in full sun and keep vines fed and watered. This standard carving pumpkin produces round, uniform orange fruit with a sturdy stem. Harvest when the rind is fully colored and hard, leaving the stem attached for the best keeping.

planting.note: Sow after frost; vines are productive and easy to trellis.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and let vines run along the ground or up a trellis. Each plant sets many tiny, flat, ribbed orange pumpkins prized for decoration. Harvest when the little fruits color fully and the rind hardens, leaving short stems attached.
